content.assessment: Delaying selection to 14-16 would restructure the network's sorting points but does not by itself resolve the geographic concentration of academic tracks or the rural teacher-capacity shortfall; implementation fidelity across a network with acute rural teacher shortages is questionable, though dormitory capacity is not a binding constraint.
opportunities: Surplus dormitory capacity could be repurposed to support comprehensive-tier students needing to travel further under a restructured network
A later selection age could reduce reliance on geographically concentrated 6-/8-year institutions, potentially easing transport-access pressure on students in underserved settlements
strengths: Dormitory capacity nationally exceeds demand, removing one potential capacity bottleneck for any restructured comprehensive middle tier
Reducing sorting points could lessen the network-level effect of geographically concentrated elite tracks on catchment populations
threats: Contracting primary/feeder network and settlement-level access gaps mean the comprehensive tier itself may face the same one-hour transport access limitations as current tracks
Network capacity to implement a new comprehensive tier evenly, given centralized control and staffing shortages, is uncertain
weaknesses: Rural teacher shortages (half aged 50+, only 7.2% under 30, higher unqualified-teacher rates) threaten implementation fidelity of a comprehensive restructuring requiring broad, evenly distributed staffing capacity
Centralized KLIK maintenance structure curtails the local network-planning autonomy needed to adapt comprehensive schooling to varied settlement transport and capacity conditions
